content:
# Top 5 posts from r/MovieSuggestions day

## 1. What is the best Christmas comedy?

**Score:** 49
**Body:** Preferably something on the newer side. Or if not necessarily a top tier movie, one that was worth watching in the last 5-7 years? 

I justify do a 10 movie countdown starting December 15th and am thinking about adding Red One to the list? 

### Top 10 comments and their replies (up to depth 10)

1. Trading Places
2. Scrooged is good.
3. I actually quite enjoyed Spirited. 😅
4. Four Christmases.
5. Not on the newer side but Christmas Vacation clears everything else.
6. A Christmas Story.
7. National Lampoons Christmas Vacation. You should have put your time restriction in the title.
8. For pg Elf, for 18 plus The Night Before, has Seth Rogen and Joseph Gordon
9. Bad Santa
10. Kiss Kiss Bang Bang

## 2. Strangers Killed One by One

**Score:** 29
**Body:** I am looking for movies like Identity (2003) or Friday the 13th (1980). Anything where a group of strangers are in an isolated place and get picked off one by one by a mystery killer. There can be a motive but it's not really needed. It sounds like something easy to find but I can't think of any others and a friend of mine keeps asking me for recs like this and I have for once nothing to say.

### Top 9 comments and their replies (up to depth 10)

1. The classic Agatha Christie novel And Then There Were None (some adaptations use the now-outdated previous but not original title Ten Little Indians) is one of the originals for this concept, and has had a lot of different adaptations. Without trying to spoil too much, some like the 1965 film do have a different ending than the book. According to the Google AI summary, the 2015 BBC miniseries and a Russian version from 1987 are a bit more faithful but I say that with the caveat that I've had those summaries give me bad info before. But it might be fun to watch a few and compare.
  - That wasn't the original title. 😉
    - It just keeps getting worse the further back in time you go.
2. Off the top of my head -

Cabin in the Woods

Alien

The Thing
3. https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/And_Then_There_Were_None


If you're interested in books, this is the best thing you'll ever find on this topic. There are various adaptations you can check out too. There was a mini tv series that came out a few years ago, but imo, it was horrible unless you're into sex scenes a la Game of Thrones which don't belong in a cerebral genre like this.
4. Comedy horror, but Tucker and Dale vs Evil is excellent.
5. Bodies Bodies Bodies
6. Cube. Bit of a sideways suggestion but I think it fits.
7. House on Haunted Hill (1999)
8. I've seen a lot of these: 

* The Belko Experiment (2016)
* Exam (2009)
* Circle (2015)
* The Platform (2019)
* The Hunt **(2020!)**

## 3. LESSER-KNOWN films where someone wakes up or comes back to their home and everything has changed or is gone?

**Score:** 22
**Body:** Like someone who's been in cryosleep for decades and awakens to all their family being dead, then has to get used to how technology and society have changed. Or visiting your hometown and just about no one you cared about is there anymore (think of that heavy Cyberpunk 2077 ending). Huge time jumps.

Well-known examples across genres: Idiocracy, Planet of the Apes, Blast from the Past, Vanilla Sky, Interstellar, 28 Days Later, Captain America. I've seen all of these on the usual lists so...

**Please suggest some obscure/indie/undermentioned ones!**

### Top 10 comments and their replies (up to depth 10)

1. Just Visiting 

Flight of the Navigator might be obscure if you’re not of a certain age.
  - Flight of the Navigator was awesome though.
2. Moon (2009)
3. Gross Pointe Blank
4. Demolition Man
5. Austin Powers
6. Robocop
7. Predestination (2014)
8. Sleeper (1973)
9. If you're into older films, I watched one recently: "The Man Who Has My Face" (1951). *"A man arrives home one night to find that a look- alike has taken over his life completely."*

## 4. What are some good Thanksgiving movies to watch?

**Score:** 22
**Body:** Besides *Planes, Trains, and Automobiles*, What are some good movies from any era or genre that are ever directly about Thanksgiving or its setting is around the holiday? 

### Top 10 comments and their replies (up to depth 10)

1. Dutch
  - Oh damn, wasn't expecting that but you're right. That kid was such a little shit
2. The THANKSKILLING franchise of course
3. A Charlie Brown Thanksgiving
4. Dutch and Son In Law
5. Addam's family values - Wednesday's Pilgrim Speech is a must!
6. I liked Pieces of April.
7. home for the holidays (1995) & thanksgiving (2023)
8. Blood Freak- a Christian, biker, anti-drug, horror, exploitation film where a Vietnam veteran can't decide between a good girl and a bad girl and ends up needing to drink the blood of druggies with his giant turkey head.
9. Hannah and Her Sisters.

## 5. Mind bending, psychological movie suggestions?

**Score:** 21
**Body:** I’m looking for a movie that changes the way I think afterwards. Or just completely blows my mind. I have already seen Se7en, Fight Club, Coherence, The Game, etc. I’m looking for something I guess maybe less known, but just as good! 

### Top 10 comments and their replies (up to depth 10)

1. Memento
2. Mulholland Drive
3. The Machinist

Dancer in the Dark

Dark City

Oldboy (the original Korean film)

Let The Right One In (the original)

Audition

Edge of Tomorrow 

Jacob’s Ladder
4. Butterfly Effect
5. Triangle (2009)
6. Predestination
7. Arrival
8. Eternal Sunshine of the Spotless Mind
9. Annihilation
10. 12 Monkeys

---

# Top 5 posts from r/moviecritic day

## 1. What is the best gangster movie among these, in your opinion?

**Score:** 666

### Top 5 comments and their replies (up to depth 10)

1. Godfather 
City of god
Goodfellas 
  - City of GodFellas
    - Goodfather of God
      - Fellas of God City
  - Ive never seen city of god but hear wonderful things

Seeing it listed on the same level as godfather and goodfellas really makes me want to see it
2. Goodfellas
  - Yup. The question is specifically about which is the best *gangster* movie. You can debate which of these is the best, most entertaining, your favorite, etc.. Only one is literally “about always want[ing] to be a gangster”
    - 100%

What Goodfellas did that The Godfather (and many others) didn't, was show how being a gangster was "cool". Literally from the opening line, it's setting up the "why", when we all know how the gangster stories end.

Goodfellas is the best gangster movie in this regard.

Scarface also shows the "cool" draw, but Goodfellas did it way better, without becoming a cartoon.
3. Casino
4. Departed.

## 2. Which actor feels like they've been acting since the dawn of time?

**Score:** 439

### Top 10 comments and their replies (up to depth 10)

1. Michael Caine. He's been in everything from The Italian Job to Austin Powers to The Prestige to Batman to Interstellar. He's in everything.
2. Clint Eastwood
3. Kurt Russell
4. Christopher Lloyd is underrated AF.
5. Morgan Freeman.
6. Does Dick Van Dyke count?! (Hopefully I didn't jinx him with this comment.)
7. Gary Oldman
8. Helen Mirren
9. Danny devito was in One Flew Over the cuckoo nest 50 years ago. He still feels relevant.
10. Steve Martin

## 3. Films that touch upon emotional infidelity?

**Score:** 306
**Body:** 

Film name: A Time To Kill (this one does not centre around the concept of emotional infidelity but rather somewhat touches upon it). 

Looking for films that deal with temptation, restraint and longing rather than actual physical infidelity. Like the example I mentioned, this could even just be a brief part of the plot and not the central focus of the entire film. 

### Top 7 comments and their replies (up to depth 10)

1. Lost in Translation
  - That’s a bingo!
2. In the Mood for Love
3. Just wanted to clarify (to those who haven’t watched the film I’ve mentioned) that A Time To Kill is a legal drama that focuses on a completely different story. Emotional infidelity is a concept that is briefly addressed across 3-4 different scenes in the film but has got pretty much nothing to do with the central plot. I’m kinda looking for films that deal with such a concept in the passing and am totally okay with it not being the primary focus.
  - They had much better chemistry than him and Ashley Judd
    - >! I think it’s because neither the book nor the film seems to know what to do with the wife character. She barely has three scenes in the film and one is of them fighting over his case and how that’s threatening to harm their family. Him having a daughter is also supposed to highlight his fatherly instinct in taking up the case, making the final summation scene all the more powerful and impactful. But the wife and the daughter are barely there and in the middle of the film, they leave town (of course because of safety concerns). Meanwhile, Sandra Bullock’s law student character is integral to the entire plot and she has more interactions with him. She often indirectly expresses her interest in him. She also becomes absolutely necessary for winning the case. But I liked how they were both restrained until the end from taking it any further. !<
4. Take This Waltz
5. Past Lives is all about restraint and longing
  - I dont know how to discuss this without spoiling it. I guess I'll spoiler tag my whole post.

>!I dont really think there was any point that she seriously considered doing anything. The devastation doesn't seem to come from her missing out as much as it comes from just seeing how much he still loves her after all this time. The way the movie moves along, I never once thought it would end any differently than it did, which was absolutely perfect. She was so in love with this guy a decade ago, but they couldn't make it work, and the flood of emotion doesnt really happen while they visit. It happens when he leaves, because its a hard thing to experience. I dont know if that makes sense or if anyone agrees. If she was single though, all bets are off lol.!<

It's honestly one of my all time favorite movies.
6. Does brokeback mountain count?

## 4. What's a bad movie that you actually enjoyed?

**Score:** 133

### Top 9 comments and their replies (up to depth 10)

1. Van Helsing for sure.
It was just a fun movie. It was entertaining enough cool werewolf.
  - Peak Kate Beckinsale helps too.
2. The Hot Chick

Rob Schneider with a young Rachel McAdams in one of her first movies. It’s not gonna win any Oscars, but it definitely entertained the heck out of me and my family during the early 2000s.
3. BaseKetball…though I personally think it’s not a bad movie others would argue with me
4. I argue that it’s not a bad movie, just bad box office. But I loved John Carter
  - John Carter is entertaining as hell.  Didn’t deserve it’s fate imo
5. The Core. Bullshit science but fun and a great cast.
6. Armageddon is one of my favorite movies.  Logic flaws and all.
7. This is the first time I noticed how bad the mermaid's proportions look in the cover.
8. A lot of people hated on Punisher: Warzone. I fucking Love that movie.

## 5. What movie did you walk into the theaters with zero expectations and came out amazed?

**Score:** 133
**Body:** Trying to think of movies where I didn’t know much about it going in, didn’t see a trailer and I  came out in awe of how good it actually was. Do you have one? 

### Top 9 comments and their replies (up to depth 10)

1. The Matrix. Saw it in 99, went in blind.
  - I was under the impression that it was based off a bestselling sci-fi novel. Nope, the Wachowskis are just amazing writers and storytellers.

What's crazy is that their story was rejected so many times, and the studio that did make it originally wanted to cast Nicholas Cage as Neo, but he refused. Which I'm sure was a relief for the Wachowskis too.
2. District 9.   I knew it was about aliens and nothing else.    Waiting for a sequel
3. Napoleon Dynamite. That movie was such an anomaly before it was quoted into cringe. We had no idea what we were even going to, but we laughed like idiots through the whole thing.
4. D&D: Honor Among Thieves.

I mean i was amazed by the 2000  D&D movie, but like... not in the good way.
  - I'd love to share my thoughts about this movie with you. But we should probably wait for Jarnathan to get here. Jarnathan is coming, isn't he?
5. LA Confidential
6. The Truman Show. I was expecting a normal Jim Carry goofy comedy. It's a great film.
7. Matrix; the commercials showed you some action but no explanation really so upon seeing it in theaters it was wow
8. The dark knight.

I didn’t care for Batman begins 

Saw the dark knight 12 times in theatres

---

# Top 5 posts from r/TrueFilm day

## 1. Thought on Ralph Bakshi?

**Score:** 37
**Body:** Personally I think he’s one of the most influential artists of the modern era. I mean modern American cartoons would not be the same without him, his influence cant be understated. Not just that but he’s also made some of the most provocative, interesting, and most beautifully executed animated movies ever. Wizards is one of my favorite movies ever, not to mention Coonskin which is one of the greatest political satires probably ever and Heavy Traffic and Fritz the Cat being two of the most influential animated films ever. Not to mention American Pop or Hey Good Lookin’, which if you know the history of the latter it was supposed to be like Roger Rabbit years before Roger Rabbit. I really like him but I’m interested to see how others feel because he definitely does have some duds lol.

### Top 10 comments and their replies (up to depth 10)

1. Love Bakshi and completely agree that his influence on adult animation is not discussed enough. To me Bakshi was fighting the good fight against Disney’s reality warping machine which convinced Americans that animation is purely for children. Unfortunately he lost and with him the potential for animation to reach its aesthetic potential. Even though it’s flawed, I really wish he had gotten to make the second half of his adaption of LOTR.
  - For me, I feel like the fact that Coonskin was even made tells me he had at least one small victory over Disney, even if it was small it’s something. I mean seriously a political satire parodying Song of the South with its own unique urban setting is one of the most batshit insane ideas I’ve ever heard yet he made it work. The legend🙏
2. I love Bakshi, I think he's well overdue a boutique Boxset to start his revaluation process. American pop is one of the greatest animated epics ever made, Fritz the cat is a hilariously distasteful romp, cool world is unfairly maligned and really all of his work is misunderstood and understated (looking at coonskin...)

Nobody did it quite like him. These days he's also quite active on twitter and Ebay.

I think Disney took a LOT from Bakshi going into the 90s (look at Oliver and Company and tell me Bakshi couldn't have made that, just with way more uncomfortable elements lol)
3. American Pop is SO good until it gets to, like...the very last act with the grown up drug dealing kid who is a super cool drug dealer and has a burning song inside him and that song is....Night Moves.

Seriously though, great movie, great vision, watched it a ton in my teen years and it really struck me with its grandiosity and throughlines of theme.
4. I did a rewatch of *everything* available by Bakshi, just last year. His early cartoons for Terry Toons, *Rocket Robin Hood, Spider-Man*, etc. through his heyday of making features, and all the way up to *Spicy City* and *Coney Island*.

My takeaway is that he was a pioneer and an innovator, someone who took a lot of risks and pushed animation forward in both technique and what was allowable *vis a vis* storytelling.

On the other hand, there is a lot of cheap looking garbage in his films, as though he had great ideas but seldom had the budgets to realize them properly. Or was he just lazy? He also really did make a lot of complete duds. 

We can almost reduce his crucial filmography to *Fritz the Cat, Wizards*, and *American Pop*, and maybe add in *Coonskin* if you're interested in a hot take on race issues, and perhaps *Heavy Traffic* for some of the innovations in technique. 

*Cool World* had so much potential, but it really does suck (and it basically ended his career), and his take on *Lord of the Rings* is strictly only a curiosity for big Tolkien fans. He really bottoms out with uninspired garbage like *Fire and Ice* (although we can blame a lot of the problems in that one on Roy Thomas' script).

So... hit-or-miss for me, but he ultimately comes out on the positive side for his innovations.
5. When I was a kid, my parents took me to see his LOTR film. So the cinema was filled with adults with kids.

We were watching the "You shall not pass" scene, and Gandalf falls down the abyss. Just at that moment, an ambulance blaring its siren drove past the cinema. Everyone laughed.
6. Wizards is also my favorite.  It’s the craziest mashup of different animation styles, tones and his own varied thematic interests that should be a mess, but somehow it all works.  I can’t say I’ve ever seen another film like it.  
  
And I think that’s the main thing that fans respond to.  A lot of his films are not perfect.  A Bakshi film will often have elements that don’t work or some jarring changes in tone or visuals. But they all feel like completely one-of-a kind originals that no one else could have made.  
  
I also just kind of love the idea of this creative visionary running an animation studio where he makes feature length animated films with a tiny team and a shoestring budget.  What he lacked in money, he made up for in ambition.  
  
The Wizards dvd had a fun documentary extra where he tells stories about making the film and how he got into animation.  He talks about how his first job at an animation studio he wanted to actually animate instead of being an entry-level employee coloring cells or whatever for a few years so he just picked up his desk when no one was around, moved it to the floor with all the animators, and by the time his manager figured out what he did he’d already been animating for a few weeks and they just let him stay there.  It’s emblematic of the kind of moxie it took to get all those films made.
7. I'm afraid I don't have anything overly intellectual to add, but I do love Bakshi's films and wish they were more easily accessible and more widely discussed. I love Wizards, Heavy Traffic, Fritz the Cat, Cool World, Hey Good Lookin'... Its refreshing to look back and see someone trying something completely different from the competition and rocking it and staying true to it, even if it wasn't as profitable as a Disney animated film. That all being said, obviously his films are not perfect and have pacing issues and other problems. But dang, different is good.
8. Spicy City was a revelation to me when I first saw it in the early 2000s. I've seen other, excellent material of his but nothing has come close to Spicy City... tho ive always been a sci fi guy.
9. I feel like Ralph Bakshi is a very intelligent filmmaker/animator whose films have/had allot to say about contemporary 70’s American society and culture.

 But unfortunately he had a tendency to lean too far into the smut and edgy-ness  rather than offer a solution to the questions presented in his films.

I still admire films like Fritz the Cat, Heavy Traffic, and American Pop (*which is probably his best film*) but other films like Coonskin, Wizard, and Cool World just come across as dated, half arsed and at times incomplete.

## 2. The Green Eyed Monster: A Reconsideration of the Night-Vision Sequence in The Silence of the Lambs

**Score:** 18
**Body:** One of the most recognizable moments in *The Silence of the Lambs* is the basement sequence in which Buffalo Bill stalks Clarice Starling through the darkness, observing her through night-vision goggles.  The scene is almost always interpreted in terms of suspense or questions of cinematic gaze- the audience is forced into the killer’s point of view, Clarice becomes the unwitting object of the camera’s gaze, and the horror emerges from her vulnerability.  These readings are valid, but they stop short of recognizing the deeper symbolism.  The green hue of the night-vision imagery is not just a stylistic flourish meant to enhance tension; it functions as a visual expression of Buffalo Bill’s underlying pathology.  Consider the words of Hannibal Lecter himself: “He covets.”  Covetousness is the driving force behind Bill’s criminality.  The night-vision’s green tint therefore corresponds directly with the archetypal color of envy, indicating that the audience is not simply seeing through Bill's eyes but through the emotional lens of his covetousness.

The film lays the groundwork for this interpretation early on.  Hannibal Lecter is explicit that Buffalo Bill is not murdering women out of hatred, sadism, or sexual desire.  Bill's pathology arises out of profound envy of the female form and a delusional desire to transform himself by appropriating aspects of women’s bodies.  This covetousness is not metaphorical but literal: he wants to inhabit what he cannot be. When the film finally positions us inside Bill's point of view in the basement, the green tint becomes a symbolic extension of this psychological state.  The entire visual field is saturated with this color, and it occurs when we are invited to observe Clarice in the same manner Bill observes the women he targets.  Green-tinted night-vision becomes an externalization of his envy.

Notice the physical gesture Bill makes during the scene.  As Clarice gropes through the dark, unaware of Bill's proximity to her, Bill extends his hand toward her face.  His hand reaches out with childlike curiosity, as though he is attempting to touch her, not harm her.  The gesture resembles longing more than stalking, an attempt to reach toward something desired but unattainable.  When seen through the green filter, the moment becomes an enactment of Lecter’s earlier insight.  Bill is not just stalking.  He is coveting. 

The basement scene is more than a suspense sequence.  It is the moment in which Bill’s pathology is rendered entirely in visual, chromatic terms.  The night-vision is not simply a device that allows the audience to experience Clarice’s vulnerability; it is symbolic of Bill’s psychology.  Green, traditionally associated with envy, corruption, decay, and the supernatural, becomes the dominant register through which Clarice is framed.  The viewer sees her not as Bill’s next victim, but as the embodiment of what he wishes he were.  The scene’s terror is the result not only from the empathy we have for Clarice, but from the realization that we are briefly occupying the perceptual world of a man whose desire and monstrousness have fused into a single, covetous gaze.

Despite the scene’s prominence, this particular dimension of the scene has received little attention in critical analyses of the film.  Discussions tend to focus on gender, serial-killer tropes, or the mechanics of power and gaze, but, as far as I am able to tell from a review of the literature, the chromatic dimension of the scene has not previously been connected to Bill’s covetousness.  Yet the connection is clear.  Lecter names envy as the root of Bill’s behavior, and one of the film’s most iconic moments makes that envy explicit through its use of color.  The night-vision sequence, often treated as a purely technical or atmospheric device, is in fact one of the most revealing psychological images in the film.  It shows Clarice as Bill sees her: something he can never become.

### Top 2 comments and their replies (up to depth 10)

1. I like this critique cuz I am a grumpy realist and always said "why the fuck didn't bill just kill her when he had the upper hand". But this makes sense, he was deep in his pathology, not thinking of self preservation or dominance, but just fully luxuriating in his envy. He was weak in this moment, so Clarice was able to get a shot off before him. Nice.
  - Glad you dig it. :\]
2. I thought of it similarly. Buffalo Bill is so sloppy and so impulsive that he got caught by a rookie FBI agent knocking at the door answering questions. Also, he invited her in his house, he’s so impulsive he can’t see what a bad idea it is to do that. She comes in and sees the moths, sewing stuff and Nazi paraphernalia before eventually putting 6 .38 rounds into him. His pathology is so bad that in the end he can’t even be an effective serial killer. He’s not calculated like Hannibal, he’s crazy. 

What makes the movie so great and terrifying, and that scene in particular, is that Clarice now sees the full extent of his pathology. He’s invited her into his house. Jodie Foster does an excellent job of realizing, the expression on her face is excellent acting. She knows he’s the guy but has no clue what happens next.

## 3. Casual Discussion Thread (November 22, 2025)

**Score:** 1
**Body:** General Discussion threads threads are meant for more casual chat; a  place to break most of the frontpage rules. Feel free to ask for  recommendations, lists, homework help; plug your site or video essay;  discuss tv here, or any such thing.

**There is no 180-character minimum for top-level comments in this thread.**

Sincerely,

David

### Top 0 comments and their replies (up to depth 10)


## 4. One Battle After Another.....More Like One Latte After Another

**Score:** 0
**Body:**   
One Battle After Another might live on in film history, but not as a great movie. It is a terrible film, and a perfect representation of what passes for “politics” in our current era.

The film is incoherent, simplistic, and painfully, painfully naive. It works, for many, because its audience is even more incoherent, simplistic, and painfully naive.

It is a film about “revolutionaries” where there is no real ideologue. The two supposed pillars, Perfidia and Lockjaw, do not actually believe in anything. There is a world of struggle, but no clear stakes. The most committed “revolutionary,” Perfidia, is actually a sexual predator. She sexually assaults Lockjaw at the start of the movie. This is presented as fine, however, because Lockjaw is also a sexually warped predator who is driven purely by his own urges toward dominance. Neither of them has any actual commitment to their “side.” They are both just motivated by getting off.

So we get this Freudian, libidinal explanation as the core matrix for these two seemingly opposite forces at the start of the movie. Fine. That could work. But it is dropped into such a horribly underdeveloped world that it never lands.

The revolutionary underground is liberating detained migrants. They supposedly do not believe in borders or states. If they are an anarchist movement, nothing is done to develop that ideology or explain it. Even in the case of Perfidia, her rejection of motherhood is reduced to a kind of selfishness, never to any belief structure that might at least help explain or justify her behavior.

But here is the big problem: Paul Thomas Anderson does not set this world simply as a group of burned out, countercultural hippies. I know it is based on a novel, and I imagine it was inspired by revolutionary cliques like the Weather Underground. The problem is that the Weather Underground existed in a very different context:

1. The revolution never came.
2. Capitalism never went through an insurmountable crisis. Racial division decreased in key ways, and the system adapted.

We are not living in a world where a small group of highly committed revolutionaries are still fighting for a world that will never arrive. Instead, we as the audience are placed into a world that is supposedly filled with authoritarianism and racism, but in the most unclear and shallow way possible.

It is truly laughable. A white supremacist structure is apparently in charge of the country at the start of the movie. Sixteen years later, it is still there. It is militarized. It seems to act with total impunity. Yet there is still some endless sea of hidden migrants being moved around. Where? Why? For what purpose? The film never gives us anything solid.

When I finished watching, my only conclusion was that it was made by a morally empty rich kid. Someone who has gotten everything he has ever wanted, never really struggled, and whose biggest complaint in life is how many pumps of vanilla go into his latte.

Anderson’s entire picture is incoherent because he is, at his core, not political. There is nothing truly at stake for him. He grabs buzzwords like “migrants,” “white supremacy,” and “authoritarian government” and throws them into the story because he assumes they will mean something to the audience, since everyone talks about them. But he has no psychological or intellectual connection to these concepts. Emotionally, the film is dead on arrival.

I could pick apart the movie in much greater detail. From the “Native” bounty hunter who suddenly turns on the “white supremacist” 76s, to the gas chamber that Anderson has to throw in to show he paid attention in school, the whole thing is a mess. But that almost misses the larger point.

Here is an interview with Paul Thomas Anderson’s father, talking about all the money he makes for such little effort. It was enough to send Paul to fancy private schools, including boarding school, and then let him become a filmmaker:  
[https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=Gjz3WoZacVM&t=88s](https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=Gjz3WoZacVM&t=88s)

Want to know the funniest part? PTA tells people he financed his first film with his “gambling” winnings, when it was just his rich dad paying the bills. Hilarious.

One Battle After Another is the political film of the establishment. It is rooted in the hope that vague identity politics can act as a veil, letting real institutional power, money, and networks operate in the background and stay hidden.

Anyone who sees this film as truly political, as saying something real, or as challenging actual injustice-good luck to you.

aul Thomas Anderson's big psychological insight: everyone is just motivated by their sexual impulses. And those sexual impulses are perverse and based on domination. You'd have to be stupid to hold any higher values. As for the structure of inequality or exploitation, he just keeps it to the buzzwords of the day.

PTA is a nepo baby, married to a nepo baby. This is the kind of work you get when you ask a nepo baby to talk about inequality. They were born into privilege and work tirelessly to defend it. The fact that this movie was well received by “progressives” tells you just how fucked up and lacking real class ideology our society truly is.  










.

[](https://www.google.com/search?sca_esv=170749da7b18780d&rlz=1C1UEAD_enUS1065US1065&q=Teyana+Taylor&stick=H4sIAAAAAAAAAONgFuLVT9c3NCzJLTZMS0ovUuLSz9U3MDE0zTM20hLKTrbST8vMyQUTVsmJxSWLWHlDUisT8xIVQhIrc_KLAPladIhBAAAA&sa=X&ved=2ahUKEwiNsturtoKRAxU_FVkFHX3OKkgQ9OUBegQIJBAE)

### Top 8 comments and their replies (up to depth 10)

1. Is this a shitpost or did you not get that it was about community and raising your kids right so theyre prepared for anything the world throws at them. And the patheticness of white supremacist groups who think theyre cool and powerful
